steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a logical completion question. The word "Therefore" signals that the blank must follow logically from Puranik's finding: workplace interruptions can raise employees' sense of belonging and job satisfaction, which benefits both employees and employers — contrasting the opening belief that interruptions only hurt productivity.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the interpersonal benefits of some interruptions in the workplace may offset the perceived negative effects.: Directly reflects Puranik's finding — social benefits (belonging, satisfaction) can counterbalance the assumed productivity loss. ✓
  • Choice B — in order to maximize productivity, employers should be willing to interrupt employees frequently throughout the day.: Goes beyond the research. The study links interruptions to social benefits, not to productivity gains, and does not recommend frequent interruptions. ✗
  • Choice C — most employees avoid interrupting colleagues because they don't appreciate being interrupted themselves.: Introduces a motive for avoiding interruptions — a claim the text never makes or implies. ✗
  • Choice D — in order to cultivate an ideal workplace environment, interruptions of work should be discouraged.: Contradicts Puranik's finding by recommending fewer interruptions, the opposite conclusion the research supports. ✗
A is correct.
